one_line_takeaway: A five-agent GPT-4o pipeline (text/URL/metadata detectors fused by PPO, plus an adversarial trainer and an explanation simplifier) reports 97.89% accuracy and a 2.73% false-positive rate on ~4,000 pooled public emails, outperforming CoT, single-agent, and RoBERTa-base baselines mainly by reducing false positives while adding readable, expert-aligned explanations.
key_finding: On the pooled dataset MultiPhishGuard reaches 97.89% accuracy, 95.88% F1, 99.80% recall, 92.26% precision, 2.73% FPR, and 0.20% FNR, beating all three baselines chiefly by cutting false positives while keeping near-perfect recall: CoT (86.60% acc, 78.45% F1, 17.47% FPR), single-agent GPT-4o (84.42% acc, 75.84% F1, 20.47% FPR), and a RoBERTa-base fine-tuned baseline (95.73% acc, 91.89% F1, 5.13% FPR). Ablations show the URL agent matters most (removing it drops accuracy to 90.95% and raises FPR to 11.33%), followed by metadata (94.90% acc), then PPO dynamic weighting (static 0.3/0.4/0.3 weights -> 95.43%) and the adversarial agent (95.17%). The explanation simplifier improves rationale quality (perplexity 25 vs CoT 47; topic coherence 0.35 vs 0.28; FRES 41 vs 21) and expert alignment (ROUGE-1 0.59, cosine 0.82 vs CoT 0.45/0.70).
core_contribution: MultiPhishGuard is an LLM-based multi-agent framework for phishing email detection. Its distinguishing design (vs. prior single-agent LLM detectors) is decomposing analysis across three specialized GPT-4o detection agents that each inspect one email modality in isolation: a Text Agent (message body), a URL Agent (embedded links/obfuscation), and a Metadata Agent (headers, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, reply-to). Their per-email verdicts, confidence scores, and rationales are fused into a final phishing score y = sum(w_i * p_i) by a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) reinforcement-learning module that learns to weight agent contributions per-email (rather than fixed weights) using extracted features (URL count, phishing keywords, domain reputation, auth results, agent confidences); reward = final classification accuracy. Two further agents complete the system: (1) an adversarial agent (also GPT-4o) that, during training, generates context-aware variants of both phishing AND legitimate emails using five transformation strategies (synonym substitution, sentence rewriting, content modification, homoglyph replacement, polymorphic variation) to harden the detector on ambiguous cases; and (2) an explanation simplifier agent that consolidates the three technical rationales into a single plain-language explanation for non-expert users (with an optional Expert Mode). Agents are built on AutoGen. Authors claim it is the first system to use multiple LLM agents for phishing email detection.
threat_model: Defensive phishing-email detection. Adversary sends phishing emails that (a) span heterogeneous cues traditional single-modality detectors miss (linguistic manipulation in body, infrastructural signals in URLs, structural anomalies in headers/metadata), and (b) evolve/evade via domain spoofing, dynamic URL obfuscation, homoglyphs, and context-aware social engineering including LLM-generated content. The system is a classifier that must catch phishing (high recall / low FNR) while not over-flagging legitimate mail (low FPR). The adversarial agent explicitly simulates evasive attacker tactics to stress the detector during training; generated adversarial emails are kept internal and not released to limit misuse.
limitations: Authors acknowledge: (1) no ground-truth explanations exist in the datasets, so rationale quality relies on indirect proxies (readability/coherence/perplexity) plus a single-expert human comparison; (2) only ONE recent SOTA model with public code (RoBERTa-base setup) was available for comparison, limiting external validity; (3) no assessment of generalization across model families or cost-performance trade-offs (all results are a single GPT-4o backbone); (4) no user study on how explanations aid decision-making across expertise levels. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: the reported gains hinge on a self-selected recent subset of each corpus (e.g., only 2024 Nazario, only 2007 Nigerian Fraud, sampled Enron ham) with a fixed ~1:3 phishing:legit ratio, so numbers may not transfer to other base rates; the adversarial agent is evaluated only within-system (its generated evasions are the same LLM family as the detector), so robustness to independent/real-world adversaries is untested; and several McNemar comparisons (esp. on the small Nigerian set and CEAS-08/TREC-07 ablations) are not statistically significant.

datasets_used: Nazario phishing corpus (2024 subset, 402 phishing emails)
Nigerian Fraud dataset (2007 subset, 577 phishing emails)
SpamAssassin public corpus (500 'hard ham' legitimate emails)
Pooled total: 979 phishing + 3,000 legitimate = ~4,000 emails (~1:3 phishing:legit)
quantitative_results: MultiPhishGuard pooled: Accuracy 97.89%, F1 95.88%, Recall 99.80%, Precision 92.26%, TNR 97.27%, FPR 2.73%, FNR 0.20%
Per-dataset MPG recall/TNR: Nigerian TPR 100.00%, Nazario TPR 99.50%, Enron TNR 97.47%, SpamAssassin TNR 95.00%, CEAS-08 TNR 98.20%, TREC-07 TNR 98.00%
Ablation No URL: Accuracy 90.95%, F1 84.20%, FPR 11.33%
Ablation No Metadata: Accuracy 94.90%, F1 90.36%, FPR 5.83%
Ablation Static Weight (no PPO): Accuracy 95.43%, F1 91.43%, FPR 5.80%
Ablation No Adversarial: Accuracy 95.17%, F1 90.97%, FPR 6.00%
Explanation quality MPG: Perplexity 25, Topic Coherence 0.35, FRES 41 (vs CoT 47/0.28/21; No-Explanation 33/0.32/27)
Expert-alignment MPG: ROUGE-1 0.59, Cosine Similarity 0.82 (vs CoT 0.45/0.70; No-Explanation 0.42/0.64)
Context stat cited: APWG observed 853,244 phishing attacks in Q4 2025
baselines_compared: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting: Accuracy 86.60%, F1 78.45%, Recall 99.08%, Precision 64.93%, FPR 17.47%, FNR 0.92%
Single-Agent GPT-4o (whole email, one LLM): Accuracy 84.42%, F1 75.84%, Recall 99.39%, Precision 61.31%, FPR 20.47%, FNR 0.61%
RoBERTa-base fine-tuned baseline: Accuracy 95.73%, F1 91.89%, Recall 98.37%, Precision 86.21%, FPR 5.13%, FNR 1.63%
